response:
The error you're encountering is due to the WPF application relying on the PresentationFramework assembly, which is part of the .NET Framework and not included by default in .NET Core or .NET 7. When you run your application on Windows, it tries to load this assembly, but it's missing because your main application is targeting .NET 7, which doesn't include WPF by default. This issue is not present on macOS because the application is using Cocoa instead of WPF, and the WPF project is not compiled in that environment. To resolve this, you need to ensure that the necessary WPF assemblies are available when the application runs on Windows.

To address this without switching your main application to .NET Framework, you can use a technique called "framework-dependent deployment" where you include the required assemblies in the output directory. This involves adding a reference to the WPF assemblies in your main application and ensuring they are copied to the output folder. You can do this by manually adding the required DLLs (like PresentationFramework.dll) to your project and setting their Copy Local property to true. This way, when the application runs on Windows, the necessary files will be available in the output directory, preventing the FileNotFoundException.

Cousin to this approach, you can also use a tool like ILMerge or the .NET SDK's built-in packaging features to bundle the required WPF assemblies with your application. These tools can combine the assemblies into a single output, making it easier to manage dependencies without manually copying files. Additionally, you can use a custom build script or MSBuild task to ensure that the WPF assemblies are included in the output directory when the application is built for Windows. This method ensures that the application has access to all required components when running on Windows, without affecting the macOS build process.

Another solution is to use a multi-targeting approach where your main application targets both .NET 7 and .NET Framework. This allows you to include the necessary WPF assemblies for Windows while still supporting macOS with Cocoa. However, this approach may complicate the project structure and could lead to increased build times and larger output sizes. To avoid this, you can conditionally include the WPF assemblies only when building for Windows, using MSBuild conditions to control which files are copied based on the target platform. This keeps your project clean and ensures that only the necessary files are included in the output for each platform.
